perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall develop and distribute the following Wireless Products in connection with the property "ICE AGE 2": (i) 1 Java Game; (ii) 1 Java Application ('Screensaver') where feasible; (iii) up to 5 MMS; (iv) up to 10 Wallpapers; (v) and up to 5 Voicetones ("ICE AGE 2 Wireless Products") in each of the following five (5) languages: English, French, German, Italian, Spanish, with Portuguese, Greek, Dutch and Swedish to be made available where practicable.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall endeavor to develop and distribute the following Wireless Products in connection with the property "ICE AGE 2": (i) 1 Java Game; (ii) 1 Java Application ('Screensaver') where feasible; (iii) up to 5 MMS; (iv) up to 10 Wallpapers; (v) and up to 5 Voicetones ("ICE AGE 2 Wireless Products") in each of the following five (5) languages: English, French, German, Italian, Spanish, with Portuguese, Greek, Dutch and Swedish potentially available, depending on market conditions.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall develop and distribute" to "shall endeavor to develop and distribute" weakens the obligation. Additionally, changing "to be made available where practicable" to "potentially available, depending on market conditions" introduces further ambiguity and makes the delivery of the product optional.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to provide contracted goods
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the language from a mandatory "shall" to an aspirational "shall endeavor", and making the availability of certain language versions dependent on "market conditions", the contract creates uncertainty regarding the licensee's obligation to provide all contracted goods/services. This weakens the enforceability of the original agreement where the licensee committed to a definite set of deliverables, thus contradicting the basic expectations of good faith outlined in contract law that is reflected in UCC. The changes make it harder to prove that the licensee is in breach of contract should they fail to deliver on all originally agreed upon wireless products and language options.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific deliverables ("shall develop and distribute"). The changed contract weakens this obligation by using "shall endeavor to develop and distribute" and making availability dependent on market conditions. This introduces ambiguity regarding the licensee's obligation, potentially contradicting the expectation of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'good faith' in the context of output and requirements. While Snippet 1 focuses on quantity, the Law Explanation extends the principle of good faith to the obligation to deliver goods, which is undermined by the modified contract. This weakening of the obligation creates a legal contradiction, as it makes the contract less enforceable, in line with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Fox shall produce the KOH Video Clips, and Licensee shall reimburse Fox for all production costs associated with such production of the KOH Video Clips, which costs may not be used by Licensee to recoup Licensee's Guarantee payable to Fox under the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Fox will endeavor to produce the KOH Video Clips, and Licensee shall reimburse Fox for all reasonable production costs associated with the potential production of the KOH Video Clips, as agreed upon by both parties, which costs may not be used by Licensee to recoup Licensee's Guarantee payable to Fox under the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Fox shall produce" to "Fox will endeavor to produce" makes Fox's obligation to produce the video clips less certain. Adding "potential production" and "as agreed upon by both parties" introduces a conditionality to the reimbursement, contradicting the original agreement where reimbursement for all production costs was mandatory. Adding "reasonable" before production costs and including agreement by both parties add subjectivity to the obligation, potentially leading to disputes about what constitutes reasonable costs. The agreement about costs shifts the obligation to make the product, a key part of the contract, unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Unjust Enrichment/Failure to negotiate in good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the language from 'shall produce' to 'will endeavor to produce,' Fox's obligation to provide a key element is weakened. Further, making the production contingent on mutual agreement regarding 'reasonable' production costs introduces ambiguity and undermines the clear expectation in the original agreement that Licensee will reimburse for production. If Fox refuses to produce the clips without an agreement on inflated costs, this could constitute a failure to negotiate in good faith and unjust enrichment as the license is dependent on the creation of the video clips. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in their contractual relations. The modification introduces an opportunity for Fox to potentially manipulate the cost negotiation to effectively nullify their obligation to produce the video clips, benefiting unjustly by withholding the clips.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stated "Fox shall produce," indicating a firm obligation. The changed text, "Fox will endeavor to produce," introduces ambiguity and weakens this obligation. Making production conditional on mutual agreement regarding 'reasonable' production costs adds further ambiguity. The Law Explanation highlights that this change undermines the clear expectation in the original agreement. While there are no relevant scraped law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, combined with the original and changed text, indicate a shift that could be interpreted as a failure to negotiate in good faith, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The altered clause gives Fox the potential to manipulate cost negotiations, potentially negating their obligation to produce the video clips and unjustly benefiting from withholding them.

perturbation.original_text: After Fox or its assigns deducts its Distribution Fee, it shall remit the remainder to Licensee and Licensee shall pay to Fox or its assigns or such other party as Fox or its assigns may designate in writing, Royalties in the amounts set forth in the Agreement or this Amendment.
perturbation.changed_text: After Fox or its assigns deducts what it considers a reasonable Distribution Fee, it will endeavor to remit a portion of the remainder to Licensee, and Licensee will attempt to pay Fox or its assigns or such other party as Fox or its assigns may designate in writing, Royalties based on the approximate amounts set forth in the Agreement or this Amendment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall remit" to "will endeavor to remit" and "shall pay" to "will attempt to pay" weakens the mandatory nature of the payment obligations. By replacing 'Distribution Fee' with 'what it considers a reasonable Distribution Fee', Fox can arbitrarily increase this fee, thereby reducing the amount remitted to Licensee. Replacing "Royalties in the amounts set forth" with "Royalties based on the approximate amounts set forth" introduces ambiguity regarding the royalty amounts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications enable Fox to arbitrarily determine the distribution fee, and weakening the requirements for both Fox's and Licensee's payments goes against good faith and fair dealing, as UCC 1-304 mandates. Introducing 'reasonable' and 'approximate' enables the possibility of one party unfairly taking advantage of the other through financial manipulations. This means Fox may increase the Distribution Fee, without a clear limit, lowering the payment to Licensee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification of "shall remit" to "will endeavor to remit" and "shall pay" to "will attempt to pay," coupled with the introduction of subjective terms like 'reasonable' and 'approximate,' creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the payment obligations. This contradicts the obligation of good faith in the performance of a contract, as stated in UCC 1-304. The altered text enables Fox to potentially manipulate the distribution fee and royalty amounts, potentially taking unfair advantage of the Licensee, which violates the good faith requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement,' directly supporting the assessment that the changes undermine this obligation.
